coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent non-conjugated polymer dots for Fe3+ detection and temperature sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yecang
Zhou, Xin
Xu, Keke
Dong, Xuemei
coredata.dc:description: The facile preparation of highly fluorescent polymer dots (PDs) still attracts substantial interest. Here, temperature/Fe3+ dual-responsive PDs are synthesized under mild conditions via the amidation reaction and self-assembly between hyperbranched polyethyleneimine and 5-aminosalicylic acid. The prepared PDs display strong green fluorescence with quantum yield of 15.5% and 53.3% in water and dimethylsulfoxide, respectively. The PDs also possess unique features, including excellent solubility, solvent polarity-dependent emission, remarkable photostability, as well as good salt-tolerance. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of PDs exhibits a reversible and sensitive response to temperature within 20–65 °C, which renders the PDs useful as a thermometer probe. Importantly, Fe3+ ion has the specific coordination ability toward the surface groups of PDs, leading to the aggregation and fluorescence quenching of PDs. Thus, the PDs are employed as a fluorescence probe for sensitive detecting Fe3+. The fluorescent intensity linearly decreases with increasing Fe3+ from 2 to 60 μM. Besides, Fe3+ concentration in river water samples is successfully assayed with this developed probe. The non-conjugated PDs with facile preparation, sensitive response to temperature and Fe3+ may hold potential applications in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Polyethyleneimine
Iron ions
Fluorescence probe
Temperature responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solvation effect and temperature sensing of PDs
Selective detection of Fe3+ and possible mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province Higher Education Institutions of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Research of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province Higher Education Institutions of China ( KJ2016A846 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PDs. Inset: the particle size distribution histogram. (B) FT-IR spectra of PDs and PEI.
(A) XPS spectrum of PDs, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, (D) O1s spectrum.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PDs, 5-ASA, and PEI. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PDs with different reaction time.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PDs; inset: photographs of PDs under visible and UV light. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of PDs under different excitation wavelengths (λ ex = 350–400 nm). The excitation and emission slit width was set at 10 and 5 nm, respectively.
(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of PDs. (B) Effect of NaCl concentration (0–1 mol/L) on the fluorescence intensity of PDs.

(A) Normalized fluorescence spectra of PDs dispersed in different solvents (λ ex = 360 nm). (B) Photograph of PDs dispersed in different solvents under visible light and UV light. (C) Fluorescence spectra of PDs dispersed in water with increasing temperature from 20 to 65 °C; Inset: Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature. (D) Reversible fluorescence quenching and recovery cycles upon the repeated switching temperature between 20 and 65 °C.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PDs in water with different concentrations of Fe3+. Inset: Photograph of PDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ under 365 nm UV light. (B) Linear relationship between (F 0
F)/F 0 and Fe3+ concentrations from 2 to 60 μM, where the F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

(A) Selectivity of the PDs toward Fe3+. (Each ion concentration is 50 μM). (B) Effect of coexisting ions on the fluorescence intensity ratio ((F 0
F)/F 0) of PDs (50 μM for Fe3 +, 500 μM for other coexisting ions), where the F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively.
(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay of PDs in the absence and in the presence of 30 and 50 μM of Fe3+. (B) TEM of PDs-Fe3+ complexes in the presence of 50 μM of Fe3+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PDs and its application for Fe3+ and temperature detection.
Comparison of the PDs with some other fluorescent probes in the detection of Fe3+.
Determination of Fe3+ results in tap water and river water samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent non-conjugated polymer dots for Fe3+ detection and temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The facile preparation of highly fluorescent polymer dots (PDs) still attracts substantial interest. Here, temperature/Fe3+ dual-responsive PDs are synthesized under mild conditions via the amidation reaction and self-assembly between hyperbranched polyethyleneimine and 5-aminosalicylic acid. The prepared PDs display strong green fluorescence with quantum yield of 15.5% and 53.3% in water and dimethylsulfoxide, respectively. The PDs also possess unique features, including excellent solubility, solvent polarity-dependent emission, remarkable photostability, as well as good salt-tolerance. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of PDs exhibits a reversible and sensitive response to temperature within 20–65 °C, which renders the PDs useful as a thermometer probe. Importantly, Fe3+ ion has the specific coordination ability toward the surface groups of PDs, leading to the aggregation and fluorescence quenching of PDs. Thus, the PDs are employed as a fluorescence probe for sensitive detecting Fe3+. The fluorescent intensity linearly decreases with increasing Fe3+ from 2 to 60 μM. Besides, Fe3+ concentration in river water samples is successfully assayed with this developed probe. The non-conjugated PDs with facile preparation, sensitive response to temperature and Fe3+ may hold potential applications in environmental monitoring.

Non-conjugated polymer dots were synthesized via one-pot method. The PDs exhibits a linear response to temperature as well as be used for detecting Fe3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent material, possess some prominent properties such as facile synthesis, superior optical property, good stability, and excellent biocompatibility [1,2]. These significant advantages make CDs widely used in the fields of bioimaging [3–5], sensing [6,7], catalysis [8], anticounterfeit [9], and theranostics [10,11]. In this context, multifarious preparation methods and wide raw materials have been exploited to produce CDs with various structures. Non-conjugated polymer dots (PDs), as a branch of CDs, have aroused considerable interests owing to their prominent fluorescence performance [12–14].

Non-conjugated PDs generally refers to a kind of nano-sized particles containing polymeric or polymer/carbon hybrid structures [13,15]. Non-conjugated polymers and/or small molecules with hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amino groups are usually selected as reactants to prepare PDs through polymerization, crosslinking, entanglement, dehydration, and carbonization processes [16–18]. For example, hydroquinone and ethylenediamine were utilized to fabricate PDs with multicolor photoluminescence at 50 °C through self-oxidation and polymerization reaction [19]. Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) reacted with salicylaldehyde or l -ascorbic acid at room temperature to generate water-soluble fluorescent PDs via Schiff base/or Maillard reaction. The authors believe that the PDs fluorescence is enhanced by the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [20,21]. In another study, CDs with three different structures were prepared by adjusting the temperature between 100 and 300 °C in one synthesis system, one of which has the structure of cross-linked polymer clusters [22]. In these synthesis approaches, the relatively low reaction temperature is conducive to preparing PDs. Hence, it is still an interesting challenge to develop simple and mild methods for PDs preparation.

Considering their special polymer structure with numerous functional groups, PDs show unique properties such as excellent aqueous dispersibility, low toxicity, high quantum yield, extraordinary optical and chemical stability, which make them suitable as novel chemical sensors for ion detection [23–26]. Zhang et al. developed fluorescent PDs from PEI and citric acid by hydrothermal method, which could sensitively detect Cu2+ and ClO− [27]. Luo et al. synthesized PDs from polyethylenimine and glutathione. The obtained PDs displayed special selectivity and sensitivity toward Hg2+ [28]. Zhao et al. treated polyvinyl alcohol and 1,2-diaminoethane via a microwave-assisted method to synthesize Co2+ and Mn2+-sensitive fluorescent PDs [15]. Besides, some of the Fe3+ probes based on PDs have also been reported. For instance, Xia et al. prepared PDs by ascorbic acid and diethylenetriamine. The resultant PDs could assay Fe3+ with a low detection limit [29]. In another example, Wang et al. utilized glutathione modified PDs for fabricating a bi-functional fluorescent probe that sensitively detect Fe3+ and temperature [30]. Up to now, it is desirable to develop PDs-based probe with facile preparation procedures, excellent fluorescent property, and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection.

Here, non-conjugated PDs with temperature/Fe3+ dual-responsive characteristics were prepared from 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) and hyperbranched PEI under mild conditions. The chemical composition and fluorescence properties of PDs were characterized and investigated in detail. The obtained PDs display temperature-responsive behavior and a solvatochromism phenomenon. Moreover, the PDs fluorescence can be selectively quenched by Fe3+ due to the coordination between PDs and Fe3+. Thus the PDs were utilized as a sensitively fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ and the feasibility was also evaluated by using river water.

Hyperbranched PEI (Mw = 1800, 99%) and 5-ASA (98.5%) was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co. Ltd. NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, FeCl3, FeCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, and HgCl2 were all from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, N, N-dimethylformamide, acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide, and tetrahydrofuran were supplied by China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. The solution throughout the experiment was prepared with 18.2 MΩ·cm water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the PDs were performed on a Tecnai G20 TEM at 120 kV accelerating voltage. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer PE-983 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The surface composition of PDs was estimated using a PHI 5000 Versa Prob X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Ascend 400 NMR spectrometer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by Hitachi U-4100 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of the PDs were measured using a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer. An Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer was to determine the fluorescence lifetimes of the PDs. The Edinburgh FLS 920 fluorescence spectrometer was used to assay the absolute fluorescence quantum yield.

PDs were prepared with PEI and 5-ASA via a one-pot method. Typically, 100 mg PEI was dissolved into 20 mL water by stirring for 30 min, followed by adding 50 mg 5-ASA. Then, the mixture was reacted at 80 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture slowly turned from colorless to red-brown. Subsequently, the prepared dispersion was filtered through a PTFE membrane with 0.22 μm pore size and further dialyzed against the water with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for four days. The solvent of dispersion was removed by rotary evaporation, and finally the purified PDs were collected by vacuum drying and stored at 4 °C.

35 μL (3 mg/mL) of PDs dispersion and a certain volume of Fe3+ stock solution were added in 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes and then diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water at room temperature. After oscillated for 20 min, the dispersion was analyzed by the fluorescence photometer with an excitation at 360 nm. In the Fe3+ ion selectivity experiments, Fe3+ ions or other metallic ions with the final concentration of 50 μM were used. As for the interference, the final mixture solution containing 50 μM Fe3+ and 500 μM other metallic ions was examined. The same experimental conditions were conducted in the fluorescent measurement.

Hyperbranched PEI is a water-soluble cationic polymer with plenty of amine groups that can interact with organic compounds or metal ions through chemical bonding, hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interaction. PEI has been developed for fabricating various carbon dots [20,23,27,28]. In this work, PEI and 5-ASA were utilized as reactants to prepare fluorescent PDs, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , and the results are verified by TEM, XPS, FT-IR, and NMR spectroscopy. From the TEM image of Fig. 1A, we can find the uniformly spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 4.0 nm, which provides direct evidence for the generation of PDs. The FT-IR spectra of PDs and PEI are shown in Fig. 1B. Firstly, some similar peaks in their spectrums are observed. For example, the peaks at 1084 and 1458 cm−1 are ascribed to the C-O-C and CN stretching vibration, respectively. A broadband centered at 3402 cm−1 belongs to the overlap absorption of OH and NH stretching vibration, while the CH stretching vibration is observed at 2924 and 2854 cm−1. However, the FT-IR spectrum of PDs displays two different peaks at 1670 and 1543 cm−1 in comparison with that of PEI, which are ascribed to the characteristic vibrational modes of amide I and II, respectively [31,32]. Besides, some new peaks at 8.30 and 6.6–7.8 ppm appear in the 1H NMR spectra of PDs (Fig. S1), deriving from the amide linkages (-CONH-) proton and aromatic ring proton, respectively [20]. These data confirm the formation of the amide linkage between the amine group on PEI and the carboxyl group on 5-ASA by an amidation reaction.

The chemical compositions are further investigated by XPS analysis. Fig. 2A shows three dominant peaks: C 1s (284.6 eV), N 1s (398.7 eV), and O 1s (530.9 eV), with the atomic percentages 70.1%, 16.6%, and 13.3%, respectively, confirming that PDs are composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements. Besides, the high-resolution element spectra of PDs are illustrated in Fig. 2B-D. The C1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 284.3 eV (C=C/C-C), 284.9 eV (CN), 285.6 eV (CO), and 287.6 eV (N-C=O). The N 1 s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 398.5 eV (C-N-C), 399.3 eV (N-(C)3), and 400.1 eV (NH). The O 1 s spectrum involve three peaks at 530.5 eV (C=O), 531.2 eV (C-OH), and 531.9 eV (C-O-C). These results further demonstrate that the prepared polymer dots contain the amide, amine, and phenolic hydroxyl functional groups.

The formation mechanism of the PDs can be tentatively proposed as follows. 5-ASA can form an amide bond with amino groups of PEI via an amidation reaction. Besides, the abundant amino groups on PEI can generate strong hydrogen bonding with amino/phenolic hydroxyl groups on 5-ASA. These make hydrophobic aromatic nucleus incorporated into the PEI chain network, and then the chains of hyperbranched PEI collapse, shrink and self-assemble into nanoparticles [21,33]. In summary, the PDs can be facilely prepared under mild conditions.

The optical properties of PDs are investigated by the UV–Vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3A, PEI has no obvious absorption above 250 nm, while 5-ASA displays a strong absorption peak at 297 nm. When 5-ASA mixes with PEI, the peak at 297 nm disappears and a new peak at 330 nm emerges immediately, which is ascribing to the increase of π-conjugation degree resulting from the formation of noncovalent cross-linked structures. Moreover, a new broad absorption band from 400 to 550 nm is observed and its adsorption intensity gradually increases by prolonging the reaction time, as shown in Fig. 3B. This phenomenon further confirms that PEI have reacted with 5-ASA to form the amide bond [34,35]. Fig. 4 shows that the PDs exhibits a strong fluorescence peak at 506 excited at 360 nm and also the PDs dispersion emits intense bright green (inset in Fig. 4) under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. Moreover, the emission peak remains unchanged even though the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 400 nm. This excitation-independent fluorescence behavior frequently originates from the molecular states [13,36,37].

In recent years, a few kinds of non-conjugated PDs exhibiting strong fluorescence have been reported and the CEE effect is regarded as playing an important role in enhanced fluorescence. These PDs do not have typical conjugated fluorophore groups, but possess sub-fluorophores such as aliphatic tertiary amines or heteroatom-containing double bonds (CO, CN, NO) [21,23]. Due to the crosslinking, folding and shrinking of polymer chains, these sub-fluorophores are fixed and the polymer chains become rigidity, which results in the fluorescence emission. We believe that the fluorescence emissive mechanism behind the PDs obtained in this work is similar as well. The covalent bond crosslinking and hydrogen-bond interaction make the sub-fluorophores immobilized and suppress the vibration and rotation of the aromatic nucleus as well as cross-linked PEI. Thus, the PDs possess excellent fluorescence performance [31].

To obtain the best optical properties of PDs, we optimize the preparation conditions including the reaction time, reaction temperature, and the ratio of reactants. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption intensity increases with reaction time at 60, 80, and 100 °C, respectively, and the most obvious increase is observed at 80 °C at the same reaction time. The strongest fluorescence is obtained as the mass ratios of PEI to 5-ASA is 1:1.5. Therefore, the above optimum parameters were selected for the preparation of PDs.

The effects of pH, ion intensity, and UV irradiation on the stability of PDs were evaluated. Fig. 5A shows that the fluorescence is very weak as the pH value is less than 4.0, whereas strong fluorescence intensities are observed and only display a small change in the broad pH range of 5.0–12.0. A similar phenomenon has also been reported by other researchers [31,38]. These phenomena are results from the amino group protonation-deprotonation, and also underline the role of the hydrogen bond in the PDs fluorescence. In an acidic environment, the protonation of the amino group leads to the dissociation of the hydrogen bond, which weakens the intramolecular interaction, and results in strong fluorescence decay. On the contrary, the deprotonation of the amino group under neutral or basic condition is favorable to form a hydrogen bond and increase the rigidity of the polymer network structure. As for the effect of ionic strength on the PDs, Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescence intensity has little change over a broad NaCl concentration range from 0 to 1 mol/L, suggesting good stability in high salinity solution. Furthermore, if the PDs dispersion are continuously irradiated under 365 nm UV light for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged (Fig.S3), indicating their excellent resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvation effect and temperature sensing of PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the polymer structure and abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface, the PDs probably could disperse in different organic solvents except it possesses good dispersion in aqueous solutions. Thus, the solvation effect on the fluorescence of PDs was studied. The same excitation-independent characters in various solvents are observed (Fig. S4), while their emission wavelengths are different. As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the emission peaks located at 506, 505, 502, 499, 482, 488, 488 and 491 nm correspond to the PDs dispersed in water, methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran, N, N-dimethyl formamide, and dimethyl sulfoxide, respectively. Correspondingly, the fluorescent color under a 365 nm UV light irradiation undergoes an obvious change from the dark green to bright green (Fig. 6B). On the whole, the emission wavelength of the PDs redshifts as the solvent polarity increases. This solvent-dependent fluorescence emission is due to the fact that the PDs dispersed in different solvents have different static dielectric constants and refractive indexes [34,39–42]. Moreover, the PDs possess good QYs in these solvents (Table S1), especially exhibits the highest QY of 53.3% in DMSO. The excellent dispersity and the extraordinarily strong fluorescence in common solvents will make PDs potentially useful for chemical sensors, bioimaging, and fluorescent ink.

The influence of temperature on the fluorescence of PDs in water illustrates in Fig. 6C. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing temperature from 20 to 65 °C and a linear eq. F = -19.29 T + 3232.3 (R 2 = 0.99, inset of Fig. 6A) is achieved through a linear fitting. This sensitive temperature response may be due to the fact that at high temperatures, the rotation and vibration of sub-fluorophore groups enhance, resulting in the increasing of the non-radiative processes [43,44]. This phenomenon is further confirmed that the CEE effect enhances the fluorescence of PDs [45]. What's more, when the sample is cooled back to 20 °C, the fluorescence recovers rapidly, as illustrated in Fig. 6D. The fluorescence intensity alternately changes upon switching temperature between 20 and 65 °C and has little change after seven cycles of the heating-cooling process. These results indicate that the PDs dispersed in water possess good temperature sensitivity and temperature-switched reversibility and thus could be served as a thermometer probe [30,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Fe3+ and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Previous studies have demonstrated that Fe3+ has a strong affinity toward the nitrogen or oxygen-containing functional group [26,29,47]. The obtained PDs as discussed above contain abundant amino and phenolic hydroxyl groups. Accordingly, the fluorescence of PDs in water may be changed in the presence of Fe3+. Given this idea, various amount of Fe3+ was dripped into PDs dispersion and the results are shown in Fig. 7A. As the Fe3+ concentration increase, the fluorescence intensity declines gradually. Correspondingly, the fluorescent color of PDs under 365 nm UV light becomes shallow gradually (Inset of Fig. 7A). By fitting the fluorescence intensity ratio ((F 0
F)/F 0) versus the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 2–60 μM, an excellent linear correlation with a R 2 = 0.993 is obtained (Fig. 7B). The detection limit calculated by the 3σ/k is 0.36 μM. Compared with other reported fluorescent probes, the presented PDs show the more excellent ability for the detection of Fe3+ (Table 1 ). The time-dependent fluorescence response of the PDs to Fe3+ is evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly after the addition of Fe3+, and strikes a balance within 1 min, implying that the detection process can be completed quickly.

Next, various metal ions were added into the PDs dispersion to estimate the selectivity of this fluorescence probe toward Fe3+ under the same experimental conditions. The detailed (F 0
F)/F 0 results are displayed in Fig. 8A. Clearly, Fe3+ ion can significantly cause the fluorescence of PDs quench, whereas other metal ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Hg2+) do not induce obvious change. Moreover, even if the 12 metal ions (final concentration 500 μM) were added into the PDs dispersion containing 50 μM Fe3+, respectively, the fluorescent intensity ratio is almost identical to the sample with Fe3+ only (Fig. 8B). All these results manifest that the present PDs-based probe has specific selectivity toward Fe3+.

The practicability of this fluorescence probe was further tested by quantitative analyzing Fe3+ in the tap water and the Changjiang River (Wuhu, China). The real water samples were purified by centrifuging for 5 min at 8000 rpm and filtering through 0.22 μm membrane. Thereafter, the real samples were spiked with different amount of Fe3+. As illustrated in Table 2 , the analytical results are close to the added concentration of Fe3+. The recoveries are varied from 92.75 to 103.26% and the relative standard deviations are 2.02–8.78%, confirming that the reliability and applicability of the developed probe for sensing Fe3+ in the actual samples.

To elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, several control experiments were conducted to investigate PDs properties with and without Fe3+. As indicated in Fig. 9A, the fluorescence emission decay curves with 0, 30, 50 μM of Fe3+ show no significant changes, and the corresponding fluorescence lifetime is 3.40, 3.34, and 3.37 ns, respectively. These data suggest that a static quenching might occur in this system, signifying a complex formation between PDs and Fe3+ [2,58–60]. UV–vis spectra depict that the peak at 330 nm is blue-shifted and the absorption intensity is enhanced with increasing Fe3+ concentration, revealing that bonding states between Fe3+ and PDs have been formed (Fig. S6). In addition, we can observe a new peak at 1045 cm−1 assigned to C-O-Fe3+ with the addition of 30 μM Fe3+ (Fig. S7). From Fig. 9B, we can find that spindle-shaped aggregates are formed in the presence of Fe3+. The corresponding EDX spectrum suggests that the aggregates consist of C, N, O, and Fe atoms (Fig. S8). Judging from these results, we infer that the Fe3+ is prone to coordinate with functional groups of PDs to generate a nonfluorescent complex, which induces the PDs aggregation and subsequently fluorescence quenching [51].

In summary, non-conjugated PDs with excitation independent emission have been facilely fabricated under mild conditions. The PDs are characteristic of high QYs, excellent water dispersibility, solvent polarity-dependent emission, high photostability, as well as good salt-tolerance. Besides, the PDs show sensitive temperature-response behavior in the range of 20–65 °C. More importantly, the PDs utilized as a turn-ff fluorescence probe can sensitively, selectively, and quantificationally detect Fe3+ ions. The quenching mechanism is attributed to the static quenching effect induced by the complex formation between Fe3+and PDs. On account of the facile preparation, good solvent solubility, and excellent fluorescence, the non-conjugated PDs have potential applications for accurate detecting temperature and Fe3+.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Yecang Tang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xin Zhou: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Keke Xu: Validation. Xuemei Dong: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province Higher Education Institutions of China (KJ2016A846).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118626.
